text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer — Xtandi 6-Month Assessment

ATTENDING: Medical Oncology
PATIENT: Robert Delacroix
AGE: 71 years

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT
Routine follow-up for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) on Xtandi. 6-month on-treatment assessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Delacroix is a 71-year-old French-Canadian retired civil engineer with a history of Stage IVB (cT3bN1M1b) high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5) diagnosed approximately 3 years ago. At diagnosis, PSA was 87.4 ng/mL with osseous metastases in the pelvis, lumbar spine (L2-L4), and right femur, as well as bilateral seminal vesicle invasion and pelvic nodal involvement. He was started on ADT with leup acetate (Lupron Depot) plus abi acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID for mCSPC, achieving a PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at approximately 9 months.

He developed biochemical progression consistent with castration-resistant disease approximately 14 months into Zytiga/prednisone, with PSA rising through 1.8 → 3.4 → 6.7 ng/mL over 3 months while remaining confirmed castrate. Of note, germline NGS performed at diagnosis identified a pathogenic BRCA2 germline variant (c.5946delT), with somatic LOH confirmed in tumor tissue. Somatic tumor profiling also revealed TP53 R248W, biallelic PTEN loss, CDK12 frameshift, and RB1 splice site mutation, as well as AR amplification and MYC amplification — altogether a high-risk genomic profile. Given the post-Zytiga CRPC setting with no radiographic progression at that time, the decision was made to proceed with Xtandi (Xtandi) 160 mg daily, retaining Lynparza (Lynparza) as the next planned option given BRCA2 status.

He was initiated on enza approximately 6 months ago. At the 2-month follow-up, PSA had declined to 2.1 ng/mL (69% reduction), with imaging at 4 months showing partial nodal response (right obturator node 1.3 cm from 2.1 cm) and stable osseous disease. He experienced grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 dizziness, and new-onset grade 1 hypertension managed with amlodipine 5 mg daily. Physical therapy was initiated for fall prevention given dizziness and balance concerns.

He presents today for his 6-month assessment. He reports that he has been taking Xtandi consistently with no missed doses. He feels his energy level has gradually improved somewhat compared to the 2-3 month mark. He continues to experience intermittent fatigue, which he rates as a 4-5/10 on most days, but has been able to resume some walking and light gardening activities. He denies new neurological symptoms, seizure, falls, chest pain, shortness of breath, hematuria, or bone pain beyond his baseline low-level thoracic and pelvic discomfort.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue present, grade 1-2. No fever, chills. Mild hot flashes persist, manageable. Weight stable per patient report (confirmed on scale today).
Neurological: Mild, intermittent lightheadedness, improved from earlier in course. No episodes of loss of consciousness. No new focal neurological deficits. No seizure activity. Cognitive sluggishness persists but patient endorses this as tolerable and stable.
Musculoskeletal: Mild aching in lower lumbar region and right hip area at baseline (known metastatic sites), no new or significantly worsened pain. No new fractures.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite intact. No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. Mild intermittent dyspepsia.
Genitourinary: Urinary stream mildly reduced (chronic, prior to diagnosis). No hematuria. Libido absent (ADT-related).
Cardiovascular: Systolic BP has been consistently <145 mmHg at home per patient log since starting amlodipine. No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity swelling worse than baseline.
Hematologic: No easy bruising, no bleeding.
Psychiatric: Patient acknowledges awareness of incurable nature of disease; mood has been stable and he remains engaged in care.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing elderly male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3.
Vital Signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 68 bpm (regular), RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 91.5 kg (stable from prior visit). Temperature afebrile.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses.
Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness to palpation over lower lumbar spinous processes, consistent with known osseous disease. No new focal bony tenderness elsewhere. Gait steady — improved compared to prior visit per patient and review of PT notes.
Neurological: CN II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 throughout, sensation intact. Coordination normal. No signs of myelopathy.
Skin: No rash. No gynecomastia.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

PSA: 0.9 ng/mL (nadir was 0.6 ng/mL at approximately month 5 — minimal rise, not meeting biochemical progression criteria)
Testosterone: <20 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
CBC: Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, Platelets 212 x10^9/L — all within acceptable range
CMP: Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, eGFR 68 mL/min; ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L, bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L (mildly elevated, stable from prior — consistent with known bone disease)
LDH: 187 U/L (upper limit of normal)
Blood pressure on amlodipine: Well controlled today

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Reviewed CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast from approximately 2 months ago (4-month on-treatment scan): The right obturator lymph node decreased from 2.1 cm to 1.3 cm. No new visceral lesions. Known osseous disease with sclerotic changes suggestive of treatment response. No new lymphadenopathy.

Bone scan from approximately 6 months ago (just prior to enza start): Osseous metastases in lumbar spine, pelvis, and right femur — stable at that time.

No new imaging performed today; bone scan due at next visit per surveillance plan.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), post-Zytiga, now on enza x 6 months.
   - Excellent PSA response: PSA declined from 6.7 ng/mL (pre-enza baseline) to nadir of 0.6 ng/mL at month 5, representing a 91% decline. Current PSA 0.9 ng/mL is a trivial bump from nadir and does not meet criteria for biochemical progression by any standard. Context: pre-enza PSA was 6.7, so current value of 0.9 represents an overall ~87% reduction from pre-treatment baseline and is well within the range expected for continued response.
   - Nodal disease showing partial response on CT at month 4.
   - Osseous disease stable, with sclerotic changes consistent with treatment effect.
   - Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant remains documented; ola (Lynparza) preserved as next-line option.
   - AR-V7 status: Not yet assessed on current treatment — will be relevant at time of progression.

2. Xtandi-related toxicities: Fatigue (grade 1-2, stable and improved), lightheadedness (grade 1, improved), hypertension (controlled on amlodipine). Fall risk mitigated with ongoing PT involvement.

3. Bone health: Continuing zoledronic acid (Zometa) 4 mg IV q3 months. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation maintained. Most recent dental exam within 6 months — no pending extractions; ONJ prophylaxis education reinforced.

4. Comorbidities: Hypertension (new, amlodipine 5 mg daily). Ongoing hot flashes from castration — patient tolerating without pharmacologic intervention at this time.

---

PLAN

1. Continue Xtandi (Xtandi) 160 mg orally daily — no dose modifications indicated at this time. Tolerability acceptable. Ongoing seizure precaution counseling reinforced.

2. Continue ADT: leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM due this visit for next 3-month cycle. Testosterone confirmed castrate.

3. Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV today (quarterly cycle). Continue calcium 1200 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU daily.

4. Continue amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension. Reiterated importance of home BP monitoring.

5. Repeat bone scan (Tc-99m) in approximately 3 months to assess osseous disease status given duration of CRPC and prior stable-but-extensive metastatic bone burden.

6. Repeat PSA, testosterone, CBC, CMP in 6-8 weeks.

7. Continue physical therapy for fall prevention and maintenance of functional status. Encourage 30 minutes of moderate activity daily as tolerated.

8. Palliative care co-management: Not initiated at this time — patient doing well clinically and functionally. Will revisit if disease progresses or symptom burden increases.

9. Discussed prognosis in general terms — patient understands disease remains incurable, focus on durable disease control. He is engaged and motivated, maintains positive outlook.

10. Return to clinic in 6-8 weeks or sooner PRN.

Time spent with patient: approximately 40 minutes, including counseling and coordination of care.
